notes: Text-only-mode SimpleQA score listed alongside the corpus's existing datalearner-sourced rows (which I re-verified and all still match exactly: MMLU 72.14, MMLU-Pro 47.16, GPQA 34.38, MATH 69.3, HumanEval 62.2, MBPP 70.43). No primary Cohere source (blog, HF model card, docs.cohere.com/docs/aya-vision, or the arXiv paper 2505.08751) publishes classic academic-benchmark numbers at all -- Cohere's own materials report only win-rate style metrics (AyaVisionBench, mWildVision). Marked independent per instructions since datalearner is a third-party aggregator whose original-measurement vs. vendor-relay status could not be confirmed.

gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09, OpenAI simple-evals SimpleQA column (short-form factuality / hallucination-rate proxy) — a genuinely hard, non-cherry-picked eval OpenAI's own suite ships this model on.

'Correct' % from Table 3 of OpenAI's SimpleQA paper ('Measuring short-form factuality in large language models'). The paper labels the row simply 'GPT-4o' without a dated snapshot; the corresponding F-score for the same row is 38.4.

SimpleQA (no web) accuracy for gpt-5-thinking = 55% (hallucination rate 40%), directly read from Table 8 of the official GPT-5 System Card PDF. gpt-5-thinking is the same reasoning-mode configuration already used for this model's existing hle=24.8 row (which the system card's own Figure 2 independently reconfirms as 24.8% for gpt-5-thinking), so the mapping to this corpus entry is consistent.

SimpleQA (no web) accuracy for gpt-5-thinking-nano = 11% (hallucination rate 31%), directly read from Table 8 of the official GPT-5 System Card PDF (cdn.openai.com/gpt-5-system-card.pdf). This is the reasoning ('thinking') path of GPT-5 nano, consistent with the model's existing gpqa row also being a 'high reasoning effort' figure.
